Mysore Secret of Statecraft Preserved in Mysore is Kautilya's Arthashastra, which was lost for centuries after the fall of the Mauryan Empire. UNESCO Recognized The Rigvedasamhita manuscript has been recognized by UNESCO for its cultural and historical importance. Gilgit Manuscript The Gilgit Manuscript, among the oldest surviving Buddhist manuscript till date, preserves India’s earliest echoes of wisdom. Bakhshali Manuscript An ancient birch-bark manuscript from Gandhara, noted by scholars has the earliest discussion about zero. Ramayana in Persian This rare manuscript of the Indian epic Ramayana by Valmiki was translated into Persian by Sumer Chand in 1715 AD. Aśvaphalaprakāśa An illustrated manuscript deals with medicinal system of Ayurveda with regard to vertinary science. The Five Pillars of Gyan Bharatam Ancient manuscripts are fragile, living testaments. Here’s how we protect, preserve, and share them with the world. Survey & Cataloguing Mapping and recording manuscripts across India, along with implementing Standardized National Surveys for manuscript identification. Conservation & Capacity Building Conserving manuscripts through scientific methods for preservation while training experts to care for them. Technology & Digitization Digitizing and protecting manuscripts with smart, sustainable technology for the future. Linguistics & Translation Working with academician & scholars to bring to decode ancient manuscripts to life through translation and transliteration. Research, Publication & Outreach Studying and sharing knowledge of manuscripts through publication of critically edited manuscripts. Explore the Manuscript Heritage of Your Region Click over a state to uncover its hidden manuscript treasures. Chhattisgarh Jogimara Cave Inscription Etched deep within the Jogimara caves, this ancient Brahmi inscription is among India’s earliest expressions of love, a timeless record of human emotions and cultural life over two millennia ago. Bring the Past Alive with your Presence A calendar of workshops, conferences, and webinars. 8th December 14th November 25th October 15th September Intergovernmental Committee Session 20th Session of the Intergovernmental Committee for the Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage December 8-13, 2025, New Delhi, India. Explore. Learn. Experience. Step into a world of knowledge, tracing ideas across generations of manuscripts. Gallery Step into a visual journey of rare manuscripts, palm-leaf treasures, and timeless heritage. 01 Publications Browse curated books, research papers, and digital editions for deeper learning. 02 Manuscript Studies Search the national catalogue, browse digitized pages, and trace India’s knowledge heritage. 03 Press Releases Jan 12, 2026 Tara Prakashana Signs MoU With Ministry Of Culture Under Gyan Bharatam Mission The Ministry of Culture has invited Tara Prakashana to participate in the Gyan Bharatam Mission as one of a select group of 18 institutions nationwide. Oct 26, 2025 Abhay Jain Library of Rajasthan Becomes the State’s First Manuscript Conservation Centre A historic step has been taken in the preservation and digitization of India’s ancient manuscripts. The Union Ministry of Culture has signed a significant agreement with 17 institutions across the country, with Rajasthan represented by the ‘Abhay Jain Library, Bikaner’. The library has now become the state’s first official manuscript conservation centre. Oct 25, 2025 India Must Strive to become a ‘Praṇamya Bhārat’—A Living Repository of Knowledge: Shri Gajendra Singh Shekhawat The Ministry of Culture’s flagship initiative, Gyan Bharatam, marked a significant milestone today with the signing of Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) with 17 leading institutions from across the country. The ceremony, held at the National Gallery of Modern Art (Jaipur House), New Delhi, symbolized a historic step in India’s collective effort to safeguard, preserve, and revitalize the nation’s manuscript heritage. Oct 24, 2025 Gyan Bharatam Mission to sign MoUs with institutes for conservation of manuscripts The Gyan Bharatam Mission on manuscripts under the Union Culture Ministry will, on Saturday (October 25, 2025), sign Memorandums of Understanding with around 20 institutes across the country for conservation, upkeep and digitisation of manuscripts. pages.title: Explore the National Digital Repository safeguarding India’s manuscript heritage | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: Explore the National Digital Repository safeguarding India’s manuscript heritage | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Explore Gyan Bharatam Who’s Who Celebrating India’s Manuscript Heritage Handwritten treasures that echo the thoughts, stories, and discoveries of generations. India’s Ancient Manuscripts preserve millennia of wisdom, reflecting the vast depth and diversity of Bharat’s intellectual and cultural heritage. Safeguarded in temples, monasteries, libraries, and family trunks, they have endured through ages and across civilizations. Many of these manuscripts still hold undiscovered, undeciphered, and forgotten scripts, languages, literature, and traditions waiting to be revealed. Inscribed on palm leaves, written on materials such as birch bark, handmade paper, sanchipat, cloth, and others, these manuscripts preserve a vast spectrum of knowledge, from Ayurveda, Astronomy, Mathematics, Medicine, and Governance to Philosophy, Poetry, Spirituality, and the Arts. They are not just relics of the past, but living testaments to India’s ancient cultural heritage. Recognising the significance and fragile nature of manuscripts, it marks the urgency to safeguard this priceless heritage. The Government of India has launched Gyan Bharatam: a national movement to rediscover, conserve, digitize, and share manuscripts in the National Digital Repository as living sources of knowledge for generations to come. Envisioned by the Honorable Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi, the establishment of the National Digital Repository marks a monumental step towards the preservation, digitization, and dissemination of the Nation’s vast Manuscript Heritage. The Hon’ble Finance Minister announced in the Union Budget 2025–26 (Paragraph 84) on 1st February 2025, marking a historic milestone in India’s commitment to safeguard its intellectual legacy. To strengthen its operations, the Standing Finance Committee (SFC) has sanctioned Rs 491.66 crore for the period 2025–2031. The Vision of Gyan Bharatam The vision of the Gyan Bharatam is to safeguard and revitalize India’s Manuscript Heritage through systematic survey and documentation, scientific conservation, digitization, and publication, along with the creation of a national digital repository (NDR), ensuring its accessibility for research, education, and public engagement. By harmonizing traditional wisdom with contemporary scholarship and technological innovation, it seeks to preserve these invaluable treasures. Partners/ Affiliation To reach the goal of safeguarding more than one crore Manuscripts, the Gyan Bharatam laid down a roadmap focusing on National and International collaborations and affiliations by establishing Cluster Centres, Independent Centres, and Centres of Eminence across the Globe. pages.title: A global community blending Guru–Śiṣya traditions with modern scholarship | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: A global community blending Guru–Śiṣya traditions with modern scholarship | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Explore Gyan Bharatam Parivar Cluster Centres Independent Centres Gyan Bharatam Parivar The Gyan Bharatam Parivar is a living network of seekers, scholars, teachers, and knowledge-keepers dedicated to preserving and sharing India’s timeless wisdom with the world. Connecting members from rural villages to metropolitan centres, it embodies the principle of sarvasādhāraṇa , knowledge that belongs to all humanity. Our community thrives on meaningful connections and collaborative inquiry, where traditional wisdom engages with contemporary scholarship. Drawing inspiration from the Guru–Śiṣya Paramparā, members experience guided mentorship alongside open dialogue, creating a space where ancient insights meet modern understanding. Together, we preserve, study, and share manuscripts, philosophical traditions, and cultural practices that represent centuries of accumulated knowledge. Members engage in sustained dialogue between traditional knowledge systems and modern academic disciplines, generating new insights while maintaining respect for historical authenticity and cultural consciousness. This collaborative approach allows ancient wisdom to inform practical solutions for contemporary challenges, from education and health to environmental sustainability and social harmony. Gyan Bharatam bridges local heritage and global understanding. Our network connects household knowledge-keepers, temple libraries, public repositories, university research centres, and international scholars, ensuring that knowledge flows seamlessly between community spaces and academic environments. This integrated approach enriches both traditional and modern understanding while fostering a shared sense of stewardship for India’s intellectual heritage. Join the Community Join the Gyan Bharatam Scholar Network Register as a recognised scholar to access manuscript repositories and collaborate with researchers across India — or explore the directory to discover scholars by region, script, and area of expertise. Register as Scholar View Scholar Directory Cluster Centres Nava Nalanda Mahavihara Nava Nalanda Mahavihara (est. 1951) continues the Nalanda legacy through excellence in Buddhist Studies and manuscript research. The centre holds 224 manuscripts in different scripts (Bengali, Devanagari, U-Chen, etc.), has conserved 362,770 folios, and provides access to over 11,000 manuscripts across major traditions. Shri Laxmidhar Acharya Chakradhar Joshi Vidyamandir Shod Sansthan (SLACJVMSS), Nakshatra Vedhshala, Devprayag, Uttarakhand Shri Laxmidhar Acharya Chakradhar Joshi Vidyamandir Shod Sansthan (SLACJVMSS), Nakshatra Vedhshala, Devprayag, Uttarakhand houses 2,500 manuscripts across Sanskrit, Hindi, and Garhwali languages in multiple traditional scripts. Himachal State Museum, Shimla Himachal State Museum, Shimla (est. 1974) houses 4,631 manuscripts across State repositories, private collections, temple archives, museums, and monastic institutions, with 1,939 manuscripts already conserved. The centre plays a vital role in preserving manuscripts across Sanskrit, Takri, Sharada, Bhoti, Urdu, Chandan, Pabuchi, Bhatakshri, Pandwani, and Devanagari scripts and languages. Shrut Ratnakar (Centre for Indological Studies), Ahmedabad, Gujarat Shrut Ratnakar (Centre for Indological Studies), Ahmedabad, Gujarat holds 2,000 manuscripts and provides access to over 25,000 additional manuscripts across Sanskrit, Prakrit, Apabhramsha, Old Gujarati, and Nagari script traditions. With core strengths in digitization, cataloguing, manuscript editing, and publication of rare texts, the centre plays a key role in preserving and advancing Indology, Jain studies, Indian philosophy, literature, grammar, Jyotiṣa, and Ayurveda. Kavikulaguru Kalidas Sanskrit University (KKSU), Ramtek Kavikulaguru Kalidas Sanskrit University (KKSU), Ramtek holds 25,140 manuscripts across Old Marathi, Sanskrit, Hindi, Telugu, Kannada, Apabhramsha, Prakrit, Old Devanagari, and Dravid scripts. The centre, equipped with conservation, digitization, and documentation units, has catalogued 12,964 manuscripts, conserved 600, digitized 3,145, and conducted 12 capacity-building programs. Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute (BORI), Pune Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute (BORI), Pune holds 28,000 manuscripts across different languages like Sanskrit, Prakrit, Marathi, Hindi, Persian, Arabic, Urdu, Tibetan, and Avesta in scripts including Nagari, Sharada, Kannada, Malayalam, Telugu, Udiya, Naskh, Nastaliq, and Avesta. The centre, equipped with conservation, digitization, and documentation facilities, has digitized 15,000 manuscripts and continuously conserves its collection. Dr. Hari Singh Gaur University, Sagar Dr. Hari Singh Gaur University, Sagar holds 6,000 manuscripts in Sanskrit, Hindi, Marathi, and Urdu languages. The centre, equipped with conservation, digitization, and documentation facilities, has digitized 1,500 manuscripts, performed primary conservation on 1,150 manuscripts (46,553 folios), and conducted multiple awareness programmes, workshops, and scholarly events. Current priorities include digitization of approximately 5,000 manuscripts, descriptive cataloguing and publication of 5,100 manuscripts, editing and publication of around 200 manuscripts, and conservation of the entire collection. Assam University, Silchar Assam University, Silchar provides access to 350 manuscripts in Sanskrit and Bengali languages, covering subjects such as Dharmashastra, Purana, Karmakanda, and related disciplines. Department of Persian, University of Kashmir The Department of Persian, University of Kashmir, Srinagar houses a well-equipped library of 20,000 books, including 100 rare manuscripts and encyclopedias. The centre focuses on the conservation, digitization, and cataloguing of manuscripts related to Ayurveda, Tibetan studies, Jyotish, Mathematics, Music, Literature, and Art & Culture. The Asiatic Society, Kolkata, West Bengal The Asiatic Society, Kolkata, West Bengal holds 51,022 manuscripts in Sanskrit, Persian, Pashto, Siamese, Japanese, Burmese, Oriya, Rajasthani, and other languages, written in scripts such as Kutila, Newari, Nastaliq, Naskh, Tibetan, and others. The centre has catalogued approximately 45,000 manuscripts and digitized 13,438 manuscripts. Currently, around 20–30% of the manuscripts require conservation. Oriental Institute, M. S. University of Baroda, Vadodara Oriental Institute, M. S. University of Baroda, Vadodara holds 31,453 manuscripts in Prakrit, Malayalam, Tamil, Telugu, Bengali, and other languages, written in scripts such as Kutila, Newari, Nastaliq, Naskh, Tibetan, and others. The centre has catalogued 1,528 manuscripts and digitized 27,500 manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. Telangana State Archives & Research Institute, Hyderabad Telangana State Archives & Research Institute, Hyderabad holds 1,60,000 manuscripts in Urdu, Persian, and other languages, written in scripts such as Newari, Nastaliq, Naskh, and others. The centre has catalogued 8,000 manuscripts and digitized 7,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. SARASVATI, Bhadrakh SARASVATI, Bhadrakh holds 5,000 manuscripts in Odia and Sanskrit languages, written in Odia Karani script. The centre has catalogued 1,500 manuscripts and has not yet digitized any manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. Vrindavan Research Institute, Vrindavan Vrindavan Research Institute, Vrindavan holds 30,130 manuscripts in Odia and Sanskrit languages, written in Odia Karani and Nagari scripts. The centre has catalogued 21,000 manuscripts and digitized 21,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Rajasthan Sanskrit Academy, Jaipur Rajasthan Sanskrit Academy, Jaipur holds 9,000 manuscripts in Rajasthani and Sanskrit languages, written in Karani and Nagari scripts. The centre has catalogued 1,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. Vishguru Deep Ashram Shodha Sansthan, Jaipur Vishguru Deep Ashram Shodha Sansthan, Jaipur holds 2,500 manuscripts in Rajasthani and Sanskrit languages, written in Odia Karani, Nagari, and other scripts. The centre has catalogued 500 manuscripts and has not digitized any manuscripts yet. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. ORI, University of Kerala ORI, University of Kerala holds 65,000 manuscripts in Malayalam and Sanskrit languages, in scripts including Grantha and Malayalam. The centre has catalogued manuscripts and digitized 45,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Samskrit Foundation, Melkote Samskrit Foundation, Melkote holds 5,000 manuscripts in Kannada, Malayalam, and Sanskrit languages, in scripts including Grantha, Malayalam, and Kannada. The centre has catalogued and digitized approximately 3,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Central Institute of Buddhist Studies, Leh Central Institute of Buddhist Studies, Leh conserves manuscripts in Tibetan and Sanskrit languages, in scripts including Devanagari, Sharada, Tibetan, and others. The centre has catalogued and digitized no manuscripts yet. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Sanaathan Jeevan Trust, Kothapeta, Chirala, Andhra Pradesh Sanaathan Jeevan Trust, based in Kothapeta, Chirala, Andhra Pradesh, does not own any manuscripts. To date, the trust has conserved 211,612 pages and 810,778 palm leaf folios. So far, the Trust has completed the primary transcription of 20 manuscripts. Independent Centres Anandashram Sansthan Anandashram Sansthan (est. 1888) holds 17,759 manuscripts in Sanskrit, primarily in Devanagari script, covering 26 subjects including Astronomy & Astrophysics, Architecture, Vyākaraṇa, Ayurveda, and Mathematics. 10,550 manuscripts have been catalogued, and a revised catalogue is planned to cover the full 18,786 manuscripts. Sevadhi Museum and Indological Research Institute Sevadhi Museum and Indological Research Institute, a charitable trust, holds a major collection of 37,200 palm leaves, 307 palm-leaf granthas in Malayalam, Sanskrit, and Tamil, along with 100 paper ledger volumes and rare maps. Nagari Pracharini Sabha (Kashi Nagari Pracharini Sabha) Nagari Pracharini Sabha (Kashi Nagari Pracharini Sabha), founded in 1893 at Queens College, Varanasi. The institution holds around 25,000 manuscripts. These manuscripts now require digitization, conservation, and proper documentation. Padmashri Narayan Das Pandulipi Shodh Sansthan, Jaipur Padmashri Narayan Das Pandulipi Shodh Sansthan, Jaipur preserves 40,000 manuscripts in languages such as Rajasthani, Urdu, and Sanskrit, written in scripts including Grantha and Devanagari. The centre has catalogued 8,000 manuscripts but has not digitized any manuscripts yet. Currently, all manuscripts require conservation. Rajasthan Oriental Research Institute, Jodhpur Rajasthan Oriental Research Institute, Jodhpur (established in 1954) holds 1,24,490 manuscripts in languages such as Rajasthani, Urdu, and Sanskrit, written in Grantha, Devanagari, and Sharada scripts. The institute has catalogued the entire collection and digitized 1,20,000 manuscripts. Currently, no conservation work is required. Hindi Sahitya Sammelan Hindi Sahitya Sammelan (established in 1893). The Sammelan’s Manuscript Section houses numerous rare manuscripts and holds over 10,000 handwritten manuscripts. In addition, the Hindi Museum has a collection of over one lakh rare books, journals, and daily newspapers, preserved for the benefit of researchers. Abhay Jain Granthagar, Bikaner Abhay Jain Granthagar, Bikaner, holds over 200,000 manuscripts. The collection includes manuscripts in Sanskrit, Prakrit, Gujarati, Rajasthani, Punjabi, Malayalam, Marathi, and Urdu languages. These manuscripts cover diverse subjects including Jainism, philosophy, literature, astrology, and regional histories. Many manuscripts are rare and over 100 years old, with several preserved in traditional bindings and fragile condition. Bodha Gaya Math, Gaya Bodha Gaya Math, Gaya, holds 10,000 manuscripts in Sanskrit and other languages, written in scripts including Devanagari and Odia. The centre has not catalogued or digitized any manuscripts so far. Currently, 100% of the manuscripts require conservation. Khuda Baksha Oriental Public Library Khuda Baksha Oriental Public Library (est. 1842) houses 21,000 rare manuscripts in Arabic, Persian, Turkish, Urdu, Hindi, and Sanskrit, along with 300,000 printed books including rare journals, and 2,000 original paintings from Mughal, Rajput, Oudh, Iranian, and Turkish schools. So far, 12,000 rare books and 3,000 manuscripts have been digitized, but no conservation work has been undertaken. Adhyatm Parivaar Adhyatm Parivaar (est. in 2001) is a religious organization working mainly in the areas of Jainism, archaeology (Jain temple and idol restoration and safety), research, manuscript conservation, and cataloguing. Approximately 20,000 manuscripts have been conserved by the centre. They also assist other Gyanbhandars by preparing detailed manuscript inventories. The organization has maintained a research library for the past 9 years with more than 150,000 books. Bharat Itihas Samshodhan Mandal, Pune Bharat Itihas Samshodhan Mandal, Pune, holds approximately 40,000 pothis and manuscripts dating from the 14th to 18th century CE. The collection comprises 73 manuscript sets donated over the last 127 years, in languages such as Sanskrit, Prakrit, Pali, Hindi, Gujarati, Farsi, Tamil, Kannada, Marathi, and others, including several illustrated manuscripts. The campus includes Rajewade Sabhagruha, Potdar Hall, Library, Museum, Pothishala (Manuscript Collection), Chitrashala, Modi Daftar, and a Conservation Laboratory, supporting ongoing preservation and research activities. pages.title: Digitizing manuscripts to showcase Bharat’s wisdom and global cultural influence | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: Digitizing manuscripts to showcase Bharat’s wisdom and global cultural influence | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Global Connect Explore Global Connect Picture this: In the 7th century, a determined Chinese monk named Xuanzang walked thousands of miles to reach India's shores and carried back something precious, 657 manuscripts that would revolutionize thought across China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam. He wasn't alone. Across centuries, India's manuscripts travelled the world in the hands of monks, scholars, merchants, and seekers, crossing the Himalayas, sailing across seas, and walking through deserts. What they carried wasn't just knowledge—it was the power to transform entire civilizations. Mapping India’s Knowledge Network From the Ganges Valley to the steppes of Mongolia, from the temples of Cambodia to the monasteries of Japan, India’s manuscripts created the world's first global knowledge network. It was a systematic Dhamma Setu that connected civilizations. This wisdom radiated across the world in two forms: as lipi-bhasa setu, the bridge of Scripts, Languages, and as jnana-dharohara- the inheritance that shaped the thought, culture, and practice. The Brahmi script, mother of most Asian scripts, gave rise to Siddham script in East Asia, Ranjana in Nepal, Bhot lipi in Tibet, and to script such as Sinhalese, Khmer, Thai, Burmese, and Javanese, which shaped the languages, literatures, and traditions Through these scripts, Indian languages and ideas influenced literature, education, and cultural traditions far beyond the subcontinent. From Asoka's emissaries who carried the Dharma to Sri Lanka (Tambapaṇṇi) and other places to Buddhist pilgrims like Xuanzang and I-tsing who carried Indian manuscripts to China, translating and founding schools of Philosophy, embedding Indian thought at the heart of East Asian culture. Together, these journeys show that India’s manuscripts were living bridges across time and space, connecting civilizations through knowledge, culture, and shared wisdom. They remind us that the spread of ideas, like the manuscripts themselves, carries the power to transform societies, leaving a legacy that endures to this day. Global Imprint of Indian Manuscripts Imagine bundles of manuscripts, carried across treacherous mountains, scorching deserts, and vast oceans, travelling for centuries from India to distant lands. Each scroll, each script, was a spark of knowledge that would light up civilizations from China to Mongolia, Japan to Cambodia. Here are some remarkable examples of how India’s manuscripts left their mark across the world: The Sacred Syllable “Om” The sacred syllable “Om”, born in India as the primal vibration of the universe, finds its reflection in Tibetan Buddhist practices. Chanted in monasteries across the steppes, it shapes meditation, compassion, and devotion, creating a spiritual rhythm that resonates with the ancient Indian tradition across time and space. The Soyombo Symbol Mongolia’s national emblem features the Soyombo symbol, derived from the Sanskrit Śaṃbhu (self-created). Its flame represents eternal growth, prosperity, and transformation, echoing the concept of Agni, the life-giving fire of Indian philosophy. Sulba Sutras Ancient Indian manuscripts on altar construction contained sophisticated geometry. Some scholars suggest that some principles predate Pythagoras. These manuscripts seeded mathematical dialogues that influenced later traditions in Greece and beyond. Messengers of Knowledge Across centuries, devoted scholars and pilgrims journeyed from India to distant lands, carrying manuscripts that would shape thought, philosophy, and culture across Asia. Here are two remarkable examples of scholars who carried India’s wisdom across Asia: Xuanzang A Buddhist monk who carried 657 manuscripts from India to China (according to Thomas Watters) after a 17-year pilgrimage. His translations and commentaries seeded schools of thought across China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam. I-Ching Another pilgrim-scholar, he translated over 400 Buddhist manuscripts from Sanskrit to Chinese.Moreover, he praised India’s great universities like Nalanda and Valabhi, projecting them as world centres of learning. Global Translators Over centuries, many international scholars devoted their lives to studying India’s manuscripts. Their translations and commentaries introduced Indian wisdom to new audiences. In the West, works such as Kalidasa's Abhijnanasakuntalam, the Bhagavad Gita, and the Rgveda were translated by scholars like Sir William Jones, Charles Wilkins, and Max Müller, respectively that laid the foundations of Indology and Comparative religion. Max Müller Germany He spent his life translating the Rigveda and editing the monumental Sacred Books of the East, bringing Vedic wisdom to Western universities. Sir William Jones Britain Jones brought works like Abhijñānaśākuntalam and Manusmṛti to global readers. Charles Wilkins Britain He gave the world its first English Bhagavad Gītā, a manuscript that would later inspire thinkers from Thoreau to Einstein. Maurice Winternitz Austria He authored History of Indian Literature. Sylvain Lévi France He specialized in the study of Indian epics, Buddhist manuscripts, and literary traditions. The Pancatantra shaped world storytelling, while Sanskrit treatises on Astronomy and Mathematics influenced Arab, Persian, and European advances in Science. Their work ensured that India’s knowledge traditions were not just remembered, but actively engaged with in universities, libraries, and cultural dialogues across the world. Living Heritage Even today, manuscripts preserved in temples, monasteries, and libraries across the globe remain as silent ambassadors of India's knowledge systems. By revitalizing this heritage, India reclaims its role as Viśva Guru, the world teacher. Manuscripts are not relics of the past but living pathways of global harmony, carrying the eternal message of Vasudhaiva Kuṭumbakam, "the world is one family." Indian manuscripts are bridges connecting civilizations, transforming societies, and proving that true knowledge knows no borders. Mysore Secret of Statecraft Preserved in Mysore is Kautilya's Arthashastra, which was lost for centuries after the fall of the Mauryan Empire. UNESCO Recognized The Rigvedasamhita manuscript has been recognized by UNESCO for its cultural and historical importance. Gilgit Manuscript The Gilgit Manuscript, among the oldest surviving Buddhist manuscript till date, preserves India’s earliest echoes of wisdom. Bakhshali Manuscript An ancient birch-bark manuscript from Gandhara, noted by scholars has the earliest discussion about zero. Ramayana in Persian This rare manuscript of the Indian epic Ramayana by Valmiki was translated into Persian by Sumer Chand in 1715 AD. Aśvaphalaprakāśa An illustrated manuscript deals with medicinal system of Ayurveda with regard to vertinary science. The Five Pillars of Gyan Bharatam Ancient manuscripts are fragile, living testaments. Here’s how we protect, preserve, and share them with the world. Survey & Cataloguing Mapping and recording manuscripts across India, along with implementing Standardized National Surveys for manuscript identification. Conservation & Capacity Building Conserving manuscripts through scientific methods for preservation while training experts to care for them. Technology & Digitization Digitizing and protecting manuscripts with smart, sustainable technology for the future. Linguistics & Translation Working with academician & scholars to bring to decode ancient manuscripts to life through translation and transliteration. Research, Publication & Outreach Studying and sharing knowledge of manuscripts through publication of critically edited manuscripts. Explore the Manuscript Heritage of Your Region Click over a state to uncover its hidden manuscript treasures. Chhattisgarh Jogimara Cave Inscription Etched deep within the Jogimara caves, this ancient Brahmi inscription is among India’s earliest expressions of love, a timeless record of human emotions and cultural life over two millennia ago. The Union Ministry of Culture has signed a significant agreement with 17 institutions across the country, with Rajasthan represented by the ‘Abhay Jain Library, Bikaner’. The library has now become the state’s first official manuscript conservation centre. The ceremony, held at the National Gallery of Modern Art (Jaipur House), New Delhi, symbolized a historic step in India’s collective effort to safeguard, preserve, and revitalize the nation’s manuscript heritage. pages.text: Preserving India’s Manuscript in the Digital Age | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Publications Explore Publications Gyan Bharatam Series- I Yuddhādarśa (A Treatise of Svaraśastra); Pages 126; 2025 It is an unpublished treatise on Svaraśāstra by Viśrāmaśarmā. It is now being published as part of the Gyan Bharatam Series. It contributes to the broader understanding of the evolution of svara theories and their interdisciplinary relevance across Jyotiṣa, especially in making predictions related to war. Gyan Bharatam Series- II Sabhāvinoda (A Compilation of one thousand verses); Pages 271; 2025 It is a 17th-century anthology of nearly one thousand Sanskrit verses that cultivates eloquence and clarity of expression, compiled by Devadutta Damodara, court scholar of King Srinivasamalla of Nepal. Covering themes from Literature, Politics, Aesthetics, Ayurveda, Yoga, and more, reflecting the wit, culture, and intellectual life of its time. Based on a 1749 CE manuscript preserved in Pune, this edition presents the work with translation for the first time, offering readers both scholarly depth and literary charm. pages.title: Protecting India’s manuscripts with strong disaster management and risk safeguards | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: Protecting India’s manuscripts with strong disaster management and risk safeguards | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Disaster Management Explore Disaster Management Manuscripts are irreplaceable treasures of cultural heritage. Protecting them from disasters—whether natural or human-made, requires systematic planning, preparedness, and response. Disaster management provides a framework to minimize damage and ensure the survival of these invaluable records. Components of Disaster A disaster is a sudden or unexpected event that causes widespread destruction, loss, or suffering that is beyond the ability of the affected community to cope with: Causes Disasters can be caused by natural events like earthquakes, hurricanes, floods, or tornadoes, or by human-made events like industrial accidents, building collapses, or terrorist bombings. Effects Disasters can cause loss of human life, destruction of the natural environment, damage to private property, or disruption to public infrastructure. Duration Disasters can be relatively sudden, like an earthquake or oil spill, or they can unfold over a longer period, like the effects of a pandemic or climatic disruption. Natural Disaster Rain and Wind storms, Floods, Earthquakes, Landslides, Avalanches, Heat waves, Cyclones, Volcanic Eruptions, Tsunamis, Cold waves, Droughts, Epidemics & pandemic, Hailstorms, Technological and biological hazards, (agents - micro-organisms, insect of infestation). Natural disasters cannot be prevented, but measures can be taken to eliminate or reduce the possibility of trouble. Regardless of the many forms a disaster may take, the actual damage to the collection is usually caused by fire or water. Even when they are not the initial factor, fires and floods almost invariably occur as secondary causes of library and archive disasters. Manmade Disaster Acts of War and Terrorism – Destruction, looting, or displacement of collections during conflict or violent unrest. Water Damage – Caused by broken pipes, leaking roofs, blocked drains, or water used in fire extinguishing. Fire Hazards – Including accidental or deliberate fires, explosions, and power failures that may trigger electrical sparks. Chemical Hazards – Spillage of liquid chemicals or exposure to harmful cleaning/preservation agents. Structural Deficiencies – Risks arising from poor design, weak construction, inadequate environmental controls, or lack of regular maintenance in buildings housing manuscripts. Intentional Disasters These refer to deliberate human actions that pose a direct threat to the safety and preservation of manuscripts: Vandalism – The purposeful defacement, mutilation, or destruction of manuscripts or archival infrastructure. Arson – The intentional setting of fire to cause extensive damage to collections and storage facilities. Bomb Attacks – Explosive assaults aimed at cultural institutions, leading to irreversible loss of manuscripts and heritage. Disaster Plan Libraries and archives are vulnerable to a wide range of risks, including natural disasters, accidents, technical failures, and human negligence. A well-structured disaster plan ensures not only the protection of collections but also the continuity of operations during unforeseen events. Such a plan provides a systematic framework for preventing damage, preparing for emergencies, responding effectively, and recovering from losses in a timely and efficient manner. The disaster management cycle generally encompasses four phases: 1 Prevention 2 Preparedness 3 Response 4 Recovery Prevention The prevention phase focuses on identifying risks, minimizing hazards, and safeguarding collections through proactive measures. Key Actions: Conduct building inspections to identify and mitigate potential hazards from structures, equipment, and fittings. Establish routine housekeeping and maintenance practices to ensure resilience of buildings and surroundings. Install automatic fire detection and extinguishing systems, water-sensing alarms, smoke detectors, dust detectors, and air curtains. Use modular compact storage systems for manuscript preservation. Prepare acid-free, age-resistant archival storage boxes for rare manuscripts, protecting them from light, dust, and atmospheric impact. Install automatic fire detection and extinguishing systems, and water-sensing alarms, smoke detectors, and dust detectors of an air curtain. Employ U.V.-protected and anti-glare glass for display showcases. Take special precautions during high-risk periods, such as renovations. Provide additional protection for manuscripts on exhibition. Install environmental control and monitoring systems (for temperature, humidity, dust, and pollutants). Create security copies of vital records (e.g., collection inventories) and store them off-site. Maintain comprehensive insurance coverage for the building, collections, salvage operations, and restoration efforts. Provide security copies of vital records, such as collection inventories, and store these off-site. Do’s & Don’ts Check weather warnings regularly and identify safe zones inside and outside the building. Practice earthquake drills at the workplace, laboratories, repositories, home, and schools. Secure loose items that may fall, slide, or collide. Observe warning signs in natural and built environments. Stay informed about the weather, especially in heavy rainfall. Monitor regular weather conditions. Take precautions during heatwaves, marked by unusually high temperatures and humidity. pages.title: Faq
pages.text: Faq close close close close close close Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) What is Gyan Bharatam? Gyan Bharatam is a national mission led by the Ministry of Culture to digitally preserve, catalog, and share India’s rich manuscript heritage with the world. What types of manuscripts does Gyan Bharatam preserve? We preserve ancient manuscripts in various formats—palm-leaf, birch bark, paper—and in multiple languages, including Sanskrit, Persian, regional languages, and more. How can I search for manuscripts on the website? You can search by theme, script, region, or keyword using the homepage search bar to explore India's timeless knowledge. How does Gyan Bharatam ensure manuscript preservation? Manuscripts are conserved using scientific methods, digitized using sustainable technology, and protected by trained experts. Can I contribute or share an old manuscript I possess? Yes! If you hold a piece of India’s heritage, use our digitisation requests page. We’ll help you document, digitize, and preserve your manuscript. What events and workshops does Gyan Bharatam organize? We host workshops, conferences, webinars, and academic exchange programs to promote research and awareness about India’s manuscript culture. Can I access publications or research on Indian manuscripts? Yes—explore our Publications section for books, research papers, and digital editions. How can I contact Gyan Bharatam? Contact us via email (gyanbharatam2025@gmail.com), phone (+91-11-23315625), or social media (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, YouTube). Who can access the digitized manuscripts? The digital manuscripts are accessible to researchers, students, and the general public interested in India’s cultural heritage. Are there special collections or featured manuscripts on the website? Yes. We highlight unique manuscripts, including the Manuscript of the Week, notable works in science, literature, and more. What is the “Five Pillars” approach of Gyan Bharatam? Our work is based on five pillars: Survey & Cataloguing, Conservation & Capacity Building, Technology & Digitization, Linguistics & Translation, and Research, Outreach & Publication. How are manuscripts identified for preservation? Manuscripts are mapped and recorded across India through standardized national surveys and collaboration with local institutions. Do you offer educational resources for students or researchers? Yes. We provide study materials, curated book lists, and details about participating in manuscript studies and conferences. Can I request the digitisation of a manuscript in my possession? Absolutely! Use our “Get in Touch” or digitisation request links to submit details. Our team will guide you through the process. Are there resources available for various languages or scripts? Yes. Manuscripts are available in multiple languages and scripts, and our catalog supports theme/script/region-based searches. What partnerships does Gyan Bharatam have? We work with government agencies, academic institutions, heritage organizations, and UNESCO for projects, research, and recognition. How do you ensure the authenticity of cataloged manuscripts? Manuscripts are authenticated by expert teams using established scholarly, scientific, and archival standards. Is there a way to join or contribute to the Gyan Bharatam mission? Yes! Researchers, scholars, and heritage enthusiasts can participate via our workshops, volunteer opportunities, and collaborative projects. How can I stay updated on news or developments? Check our Press Releases section and follow us on social media for latest activities, events, and new publications. Are there privacy and accessibility options on the site? Our website offers accessibility features (e.g., dyslexia/ADHD modes, color options, text-to-speech) and follows strict privacy guidelines detailed in our Privacy Policy. Gyan Mitram AI chevron_right BharatiyaGPT chevron_right Treasures Hidden in India’s Manuscripts Ancient pages that whisper stories of numbers, languages, and discoveries still shaping our world. Mysore Secret of Statecraft Preserved in Mysore is Kautilya's Arthashastra, which was lost for centuries after the fall of the Mauryan Empire. UNESCO Recognized The Rigvedasamhita manuscript has been recognized by UNESCO for its cultural and historical importance. Gilgit Manuscript The Gilgit Manuscript, among the oldest surviving Buddhist manuscript till date, preserves India’s earliest echoes of wisdom. Bakhshali Manuscript An ancient birch-bark manuscript from Gandhara, noted by scholars has the earliest discussion about zero. Ramayana in Persian This rare manuscript of the Indian epic Ramayana by Valmiki was translated into Persian by Sumer Chand in 1715 AD. Aśvaphalaprakāśa An illustrated manuscript deals with medicinal system of Ayurveda with regard to vertinary science. The Five Pillars of Gyan Bharatam Ancient manuscripts are fragile, living testaments. Here’s how we protect, preserve, and share them with the world. Survey & Cataloguing Mapping and recording manuscripts across India, along with implementing Standardized National Surveys for manuscript identification. Conservation & Capacity Building Conserving manuscripts through scientific methods for preservation while training experts to care for them. Technology & Digitization Digitizing and protecting manuscripts with smart, sustainable technology for the future. Linguistics & Translation Working with academician & scholars to bring to decode ancient manuscripts to life through translation and transliteration. Research, Publication & Outreach Studying and sharing knowledge of manuscripts through publication of critically edited manuscripts. Explore the Manuscript Heritage of Your Region Click over a state to uncover its hidden manuscript treasures. Chhattisgarh Jogimara Cave Inscription Etched deep within the Jogimara caves, this ancient Brahmi inscription is among India’s earliest expressions of love, a timeless record of human emotions and cultural life over two millennia ago. pages.title: Explore the National Digital Repository safeguarding India’s manuscript heritage | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: Explore the National Digital Repository safeguarding India’s manuscript heritage | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Explore Gyan Bharatam Who’s Who Celebrating India’s Manuscript Heritage Handwritten treasures that echo the thoughts, stories, and discoveries of generations. India’s Ancient Manuscripts preserve millennia of wisdom, reflecting the vast depth and diversity of Bharat’s intellectual and cultural heritage. Safeguarded in temples, monasteries, libraries, and family trunks, they have endured through ages and across civilizations. Many of these manuscripts still hold undiscovered, undeciphered, and forgotten scripts, languages, literature, and traditions waiting to be revealed. Inscribed on palm leaves, written on materials such as birch bark, handmade paper, sanchipat, cloth, and others, these manuscripts preserve a vast spectrum of knowledge, from Ayurveda, Astronomy, Mathematics, Medicine, and Governance to Philosophy, Poetry, Spirituality, and the Arts. They are not just relics of the past, but living testaments to India’s ancient cultural heritage. Recognising the significance and fragile nature of manuscripts, it marks the urgency to safeguard this priceless heritage. The Government of India has launched Gyan Bharatam: a national movement to rediscover, conserve, digitize, and share manuscripts in the National Digital Repository as living sources of knowledge for generations to come. Envisioned by the Honorable Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi, the establishment of the National Digital Repository marks a monumental step towards the preservation, digitization, and dissemination of the Nation’s vast Manuscript Heritage. The Hon’ble Finance Minister announced in the Union Budget 2025–26 (Paragraph 84) on 1st February 2025, marking a historic milestone in India’s commitment to safeguard its intellectual legacy. To strengthen its operations, the Standing Finance Committee (SFC) has sanctioned Rs 491.66 crore for the period 2025–2031. The Vision of Gyan Bharatam The vision of the Gyan Bharatam is to safeguard and revitalize India’s Manuscript Heritage through systematic survey and documentation, scientific conservation, digitization, and publication, along with the creation of a national digital repository (NDR), ensuring its accessibility for research, education, and public engagement. By harmonizing traditional wisdom with contemporary scholarship and technological innovation, it seeks to preserve these invaluable treasures. Partners/ Affiliation To reach the goal of safeguarding more than one crore Manuscripts, the Gyan Bharatam laid down a roadmap focusing on National and International collaborations and affiliations by establishing Cluster Centres, Independent Centres, and Centres of Eminence across the Globe. pages.title: A global community blending Guru–Śiṣya traditions with modern scholarship | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: A global community blending Guru–Śiṣya traditions with modern scholarship | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Explore Gyan Bharatam Parivar Cluster Centres Independent Centres Gyan Bharatam Parivar The Gyan Bharatam Parivar is a living network of seekers, scholars, teachers, and knowledge-keepers dedicated to preserving and sharing India’s timeless wisdom with the world. Connecting members from rural villages to metropolitan centres, it embodies the principle of sarvasādhāraṇa , knowledge that belongs to all humanity. Our community thrives on meaningful connections and collaborative inquiry, where traditional wisdom engages with contemporary scholarship. Drawing inspiration from the Guru–Śiṣya Paramparā, members experience guided mentorship alongside open dialogue, creating a space where ancient insights meet modern understanding. Together, we preserve, study, and share manuscripts, philosophical traditions, and cultural practices that represent centuries of accumulated knowledge. Members engage in sustained dialogue between traditional knowledge systems and modern academic disciplines, generating new insights while maintaining respect for historical authenticity and cultural consciousness. This collaborative approach allows ancient wisdom to inform practical solutions for contemporary challenges, from education and health to environmental sustainability and social harmony. Gyan Bharatam bridges local heritage and global understanding. Our network connects household knowledge-keepers, temple libraries, public repositories, university research centres, and international scholars, ensuring that knowledge flows seamlessly between community spaces and academic environments. This integrated approach enriches both traditional and modern understanding while fostering a shared sense of stewardship for India’s intellectual heritage. Join the Community Join the Gyan Bharatam Scholar Network Register as a recognised scholar to access manuscript repositories and collaborate with researchers across India — or explore the directory to discover scholars by region, script, and area of expertise. Register as Scholar View Scholar Directory Cluster Centres Nava Nalanda Mahavihara Nava Nalanda Mahavihara (est. 1951) continues the Nalanda legacy through excellence in Buddhist Studies and manuscript research. The centre holds 224 manuscripts in different scripts (Bengali, Devanagari, U-Chen, etc.), has conserved 362,770 folios, and provides access to over 11,000 manuscripts across major traditions. Shri Laxmidhar Acharya Chakradhar Joshi Vidyamandir Shod Sansthan (SLACJVMSS), Nakshatra Vedhshala, Devprayag, Uttarakhand Shri Laxmidhar Acharya Chakradhar Joshi Vidyamandir Shod Sansthan (SLACJVMSS), Nakshatra Vedhshala, Devprayag, Uttarakhand houses 2,500 manuscripts across Sanskrit, Hindi, and Garhwali languages in multiple traditional scripts. Himachal State Museum, Shimla Himachal State Museum, Shimla (est. 1974) houses 4,631 manuscripts across State repositories, private collections, temple archives, museums, and monastic institutions, with 1,939 manuscripts already conserved. The centre plays a vital role in preserving manuscripts across Sanskrit, Takri, Sharada, Bhoti, Urdu, Chandan, Pabuchi, Bhatakshri, Pandwani, and Devanagari scripts and languages. Shrut Ratnakar (Centre for Indological Studies), Ahmedabad, Gujarat Shrut Ratnakar (Centre for Indological Studies), Ahmedabad, Gujarat holds 2,000 manuscripts and provides access to over 25,000 additional manuscripts across Sanskrit, Prakrit, Apabhramsha, Old Gujarati, and Nagari script traditions. With core strengths in digitization, cataloguing, manuscript editing, and publication of rare texts, the centre plays a key role in preserving and advancing Indology, Jain studies, Indian philosophy, literature, grammar, Jyotiṣa, and Ayurveda. Kavikulaguru Kalidas Sanskrit University (KKSU), Ramtek Kavikulaguru Kalidas Sanskrit University (KKSU), Ramtek holds 25,140 manuscripts across Old Marathi, Sanskrit, Hindi, Telugu, Kannada, Apabhramsha, Prakrit, Old Devanagari, and Dravid scripts. The centre, equipped with conservation, digitization, and documentation units, has catalogued 12,964 manuscripts, conserved 600, digitized 3,145, and conducted 12 capacity-building programs. Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute (BORI), Pune Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute (BORI), Pune holds 28,000 manuscripts across different languages like Sanskrit, Prakrit, Marathi, Hindi, Persian, Arabic, Urdu, Tibetan, and Avesta in scripts including Nagari, Sharada, Kannada, Malayalam, Telugu, Udiya, Naskh, Nastaliq, and Avesta. The centre, equipped with conservation, digitization, and documentation facilities, has digitized 15,000 manuscripts and continuously conserves its collection. Dr. Hari Singh Gaur University, Sagar Dr. Hari Singh Gaur University, Sagar holds 6,000 manuscripts in Sanskrit, Hindi, Marathi, and Urdu languages. The centre, equipped with conservation, digitization, and documentation facilities, has digitized 1,500 manuscripts, performed primary conservation on 1,150 manuscripts (46,553 folios), and conducted multiple awareness programmes, workshops, and scholarly events. Current priorities include digitization of approximately 5,000 manuscripts, descriptive cataloguing and publication of 5,100 manuscripts, editing and publication of around 200 manuscripts, and conservation of the entire collection. Assam University, Silchar Assam University, Silchar provides access to 350 manuscripts in Sanskrit and Bengali languages, covering subjects such as Dharmashastra, Purana, Karmakanda, and related disciplines. Department of Persian, University of Kashmir The Department of Persian, University of Kashmir, Srinagar houses a well-equipped library of 20,000 books, including 100 rare manuscripts and encyclopedias. The centre focuses on the conservation, digitization, and cataloguing of manuscripts related to Ayurveda, Tibetan studies, Jyotish, Mathematics, Music, Literature, and Art & Culture. The Asiatic Society, Kolkata, West Bengal The Asiatic Society, Kolkata, West Bengal holds 51,022 manuscripts in Sanskrit, Persian, Pashto, Siamese, Japanese, Burmese, Oriya, Rajasthani, and other languages, written in scripts such as Kutila, Newari, Nastaliq, Naskh, Tibetan, and others. The centre has catalogued approximately 45,000 manuscripts and digitized 13,438 manuscripts. Currently, around 20–30% of the manuscripts require conservation. Oriental Institute, M. S. University of Baroda, Vadodara Oriental Institute, M. S. University of Baroda, Vadodara holds 31,453 manuscripts in Prakrit, Malayalam, Tamil, Telugu, Bengali, and other languages, written in scripts such as Kutila, Newari, Nastaliq, Naskh, Tibetan, and others. The centre has catalogued 1,528 manuscripts and digitized 27,500 manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. Telangana State Archives & Research Institute, Hyderabad Telangana State Archives & Research Institute, Hyderabad holds 1,60,000 manuscripts in Urdu, Persian, and other languages, written in scripts such as Newari, Nastaliq, Naskh, and others. The centre has catalogued 8,000 manuscripts and digitized 7,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. SARASVATI, Bhadrakh SARASVATI, Bhadrakh holds 5,000 manuscripts in Odia and Sanskrit languages, written in Odia Karani script. The centre has catalogued 1,500 manuscripts and has not yet digitized any manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. Vrindavan Research Institute, Vrindavan Vrindavan Research Institute, Vrindavan holds 30,130 manuscripts in Odia and Sanskrit languages, written in Odia Karani and Nagari scripts. The centre has catalogued 21,000 manuscripts and digitized 21,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Rajasthan Sanskrit Academy, Jaipur Rajasthan Sanskrit Academy, Jaipur holds 9,000 manuscripts in Rajasthani and Sanskrit languages, written in Karani and Nagari scripts. The centre has catalogued 1,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. Vishguru Deep Ashram Shodha Sansthan, Jaipur Vishguru Deep Ashram Shodha Sansthan, Jaipur holds 2,500 manuscripts in Rajasthani and Sanskrit languages, written in Odia Karani, Nagari, and other scripts. The centre has catalogued 500 manuscripts and has not digitized any manuscripts yet. Currently, around 80–85% of the manuscripts require conservation. ORI, University of Kerala ORI, University of Kerala holds 65,000 manuscripts in Malayalam and Sanskrit languages, in scripts including Grantha and Malayalam. The centre has catalogued manuscripts and digitized 45,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Samskrit Foundation, Melkote Samskrit Foundation, Melkote holds 5,000 manuscripts in Kannada, Malayalam, and Sanskrit languages, in scripts including Grantha, Malayalam, and Kannada. The centre has catalogued and digitized approximately 3,000 manuscripts. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Central Institute of Buddhist Studies, Leh Central Institute of Buddhist Studies, Leh conserves manuscripts in Tibetan and Sanskrit languages, in scripts including Devanagari, Sharada, Tibetan, and others. The centre has catalogued and digitized no manuscripts yet. Currently, around 40–45% of the manuscripts require conservation. Sanaathan Jeevan Trust, Kothapeta, Chirala, Andhra Pradesh Sanaathan Jeevan Trust, based in Kothapeta, Chirala, Andhra Pradesh, does not own any manuscripts. To date, the trust has conserved 211,612 pages and 810,778 palm leaf folios. So far, the Trust has completed the primary transcription of 20 manuscripts. Independent Centres Anandashram Sansthan Anandashram Sansthan (est. 1888) holds 17,759 manuscripts in Sanskrit, primarily in Devanagari script, covering 26 subjects including Astronomy & Astrophysics, Architecture, Vyākaraṇa, Ayurveda, and Mathematics. 10,550 manuscripts have been catalogued, and a revised catalogue is planned to cover the full 18,786 manuscripts. Sevadhi Museum and Indological Research Institute Sevadhi Museum and Indological Research Institute, a charitable trust, holds a major collection of 37,200 palm leaves, 307 palm-leaf granthas in Malayalam, Sanskrit, and Tamil, along with 100 paper ledger volumes and rare maps. Nagari Pracharini Sabha (Kashi Nagari Pracharini Sabha) Nagari Pracharini Sabha (Kashi Nagari Pracharini Sabha), founded in 1893 at Queens College, Varanasi. The institution holds around 25,000 manuscripts. These manuscripts now require digitization, conservation, and proper documentation. Padmashri Narayan Das Pandulipi Shodh Sansthan, Jaipur Padmashri Narayan Das Pandulipi Shodh Sansthan, Jaipur preserves 40,000 manuscripts in languages such as Rajasthani, Urdu, and Sanskrit, written in scripts including Grantha and Devanagari. The centre has catalogued 8,000 manuscripts but has not digitized any manuscripts yet. Currently, all manuscripts require conservation. Rajasthan Oriental Research Institute, Jodhpur Rajasthan Oriental Research Institute, Jodhpur (established in 1954) holds 1,24,490 manuscripts in languages such as Rajasthani, Urdu, and Sanskrit, written in Grantha, Devanagari, and Sharada scripts. The institute has catalogued the entire collection and digitized 1,20,000 manuscripts. Currently, no conservation work is required. Hindi Sahitya Sammelan Hindi Sahitya Sammelan (established in 1893). The Sammelan’s Manuscript Section houses numerous rare manuscripts and holds over 10,000 handwritten manuscripts. In addition, the Hindi Museum has a collection of over one lakh rare books, journals, and daily newspapers, preserved for the benefit of researchers. Abhay Jain Granthagar, Bikaner Abhay Jain Granthagar, Bikaner, holds over 200,000 manuscripts. The collection includes manuscripts in Sanskrit, Prakrit, Gujarati, Rajasthani, Punjabi, Malayalam, Marathi, and Urdu languages. These manuscripts cover diverse subjects including Jainism, philosophy, literature, astrology, and regional histories. Many manuscripts are rare and over 100 years old, with several preserved in traditional bindings and fragile condition. Bodha Gaya Math, Gaya Bodha Gaya Math, Gaya, holds 10,000 manuscripts in Sanskrit and other languages, written in scripts including Devanagari and Odia. The centre has not catalogued or digitized any manuscripts so far. Currently, 100% of the manuscripts require conservation. Khuda Baksha Oriental Public Library Khuda Baksha Oriental Public Library (est. 1842) houses 21,000 rare manuscripts in Arabic, Persian, Turkish, Urdu, Hindi, and Sanskrit, along with 300,000 printed books including rare journals, and 2,000 original paintings from Mughal, Rajput, Oudh, Iranian, and Turkish schools. So far, 12,000 rare books and 3,000 manuscripts have been digitized, but no conservation work has been undertaken. Adhyatm Parivaar Adhyatm Parivaar (est. in 2001) is a religious organization working mainly in the areas of Jainism, archaeology (Jain temple and idol restoration and safety), research, manuscript conservation, and cataloguing. Approximately 20,000 manuscripts have been conserved by the centre. They also assist other Gyanbhandars by preparing detailed manuscript inventories. The organization has maintained a research library for the past 9 years with more than 150,000 books. Bharat Itihas Samshodhan Mandal, Pune Bharat Itihas Samshodhan Mandal, Pune, holds approximately 40,000 pothis and manuscripts dating from the 14th to 18th century CE. The collection comprises 73 manuscript sets donated over the last 127 years, in languages such as Sanskrit, Prakrit, Pali, Hindi, Gujarati, Farsi, Tamil, Kannada, Marathi, and others, including several illustrated manuscripts. The campus includes Rajewade Sabhagruha, Potdar Hall, Library, Museum, Pothishala (Manuscript Collection), Chitrashala, Modi Daftar, and a Conservation Laboratory, supporting ongoing preservation and research activities. pages.title: Digitizing manuscripts to showcase Bharat’s wisdom and global cultural influence | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: Digitizing manuscripts to showcase Bharat’s wisdom and global cultural influence | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Global Connect Explore Global Connect Picture this: In the 7th century, a determined Chinese monk named Xuanzang walked thousands of miles to reach India's shores and carried back something precious, 657 manuscripts that would revolutionize thought across China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam. He wasn't alone. Across centuries, India's manuscripts travelled the world in the hands of monks, scholars, merchants, and seekers, crossing the Himalayas, sailing across seas, and walking through deserts. What they carried wasn't just knowledge—it was the power to transform entire civilizations. Mapping India’s Knowledge Network From the Ganges Valley to the steppes of Mongolia, from the temples of Cambodia to the monasteries of Japan, India’s manuscripts created the world's first global knowledge network. It was a systematic Dhamma Setu that connected civilizations. This wisdom radiated across the world in two forms: as lipi-bhasa setu, the bridge of Scripts, Languages, and as jnana-dharohara- the inheritance that shaped the thought, culture, and practice. The Brahmi script, mother of most Asian scripts, gave rise to Siddham script in East Asia, Ranjana in Nepal, Bhot lipi in Tibet, and to script such as Sinhalese, Khmer, Thai, Burmese, and Javanese, which shaped the languages, literatures, and traditions Through these scripts, Indian languages and ideas influenced literature, education, and cultural traditions far beyond the subcontinent. From Asoka's emissaries who carried the Dharma to Sri Lanka (Tambapaṇṇi) and other places to Buddhist pilgrims like Xuanzang and I-tsing who carried Indian manuscripts to China, translating and founding schools of Philosophy, embedding Indian thought at the heart of East Asian culture. Together, these journeys show that India’s manuscripts were living bridges across time and space, connecting civilizations through knowledge, culture, and shared wisdom. They remind us that the spread of ideas, like the manuscripts themselves, carries the power to transform societies, leaving a legacy that endures to this day. Global Imprint of Indian Manuscripts Imagine bundles of manuscripts, carried across treacherous mountains, scorching deserts, and vast oceans, travelling for centuries from India to distant lands. Each scroll, each script, was a spark of knowledge that would light up civilizations from China to Mongolia, Japan to Cambodia. Here are some remarkable examples of how India’s manuscripts left their mark across the world: The Sacred Syllable “Om” The sacred syllable “Om”, born in India as the primal vibration of the universe, finds its reflection in Tibetan Buddhist practices. Chanted in monasteries across the steppes, it shapes meditation, compassion, and devotion, creating a spiritual rhythm that resonates with the ancient Indian tradition across time and space. The Soyombo Symbol Mongolia’s national emblem features the Soyombo symbol, derived from the Sanskrit Śaṃbhu (self-created). Its flame represents eternal growth, prosperity, and transformation, echoing the concept of Agni, the life-giving fire of Indian philosophy. Sulba Sutras Ancient Indian manuscripts on altar construction contained sophisticated geometry. Some scholars suggest that some principles predate Pythagoras. These manuscripts seeded mathematical dialogues that influenced later traditions in Greece and beyond. Messengers of Knowledge Across centuries, devoted scholars and pilgrims journeyed from India to distant lands, carrying manuscripts that would shape thought, philosophy, and culture across Asia. Here are two remarkable examples of scholars who carried India’s wisdom across Asia: Xuanzang A Buddhist monk who carried 657 manuscripts from India to China (according to Thomas Watters) after a 17-year pilgrimage. His translations and commentaries seeded schools of thought across China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam. I-Ching Another pilgrim-scholar, he translated over 400 Buddhist manuscripts from Sanskrit to Chinese.Moreover, he praised India’s great universities like Nalanda and Valabhi, projecting them as world centres of learning. Global Translators Over centuries, many international scholars devoted their lives to studying India’s manuscripts. Their translations and commentaries introduced Indian wisdom to new audiences. In the West, works such as Kalidasa's Abhijnanasakuntalam, the Bhagavad Gita, and the Rgveda were translated by scholars like Sir William Jones, Charles Wilkins, and Max Müller, respectively that laid the foundations of Indology and Comparative religion. Max Müller Germany He spent his life translating the Rigveda and editing the monumental Sacred Books of the East, bringing Vedic wisdom to Western universities. Sir William Jones Britain Jones brought works like Abhijñānaśākuntalam and Manusmṛti to global readers. Charles Wilkins Britain He gave the world its first English Bhagavad Gītā, a manuscript that would later inspire thinkers from Thoreau to Einstein. Maurice Winternitz Austria He authored History of Indian Literature. Sylvain Lévi France He specialized in the study of Indian epics, Buddhist manuscripts, and literary traditions. The Pancatantra shaped world storytelling, while Sanskrit treatises on Astronomy and Mathematics influenced Arab, Persian, and European advances in Science. Their work ensured that India’s knowledge traditions were not just remembered, but actively engaged with in universities, libraries, and cultural dialogues across the world. Living Heritage Even today, manuscripts preserved in temples, monasteries, and libraries across the globe remain as silent ambassadors of India's knowledge systems. By revitalizing this heritage, India reclaims its role as Viśva Guru, the world teacher. Manuscripts are not relics of the past but living pathways of global harmony, carrying the eternal message of Vasudhaiva Kuṭumbakam, "the world is one family." Indian manuscripts are bridges connecting civilizations, transforming societies, and proving that true knowledge knows no borders. It is now being published as part of the Gyan Bharatam Series. It contributes to the broader understanding of the evolution of svara theories and their interdisciplinary relevance across Jyotiṣa, especially in making predictions related to war. Gyan Bharatam Series- II Sabhāvinoda (A Compilation of one thousand verses); Pages 271; 2025 It is a 17th-century anthology of nearly one thousand Sanskrit verses that cultivates eloquence and clarity of expression, compiled by Devadutta Damodara, court scholar of King Srinivasamalla of Nepal. Covering themes from Literature, Politics, Aesthetics, Ayurveda, Yoga, and more, reflecting the wit, culture, and intellectual life of its time. Based on a 1749 CE manuscript preserved in Pune, this edition presents the work with translation for the first time, offering readers both scholarly depth and literary charm. pages.title: Protecting India’s manuscripts with strong disaster management and risk safeguards | Gyan Bharatam
pages.text: Protecting India’s manuscripts with strong disaster management and risk safeguards | Gyan Bharatam close close close close close close Disaster Management Explore Disaster Management Manuscripts are irreplaceable treasures of cultural heritage. Protecting them from disasters—whether natural or human-made, requires systematic planning, preparedness, and response. Disaster management provides a framework to minimize damage and ensure the survival of these invaluable records. Components of Disaster A disaster is a sudden or unexpected event that causes widespread destruction, loss, or suffering that is beyond the ability of the affected community to cope with: Causes Disasters can be caused by natural events like earthquakes, hurricanes, floods, or tornadoes, or by human-made events like industrial accidents, building collapses, or terrorist bombings. Effects Disasters can cause loss of human life, destruction of the natural environment, damage to private property, or disruption to public infrastructure. Duration Disasters can be relatively sudden, like an earthquake or oil spill, or they can unfold over a longer period, like the effects of a pandemic or climatic disruption. Natural Disaster Rain and Wind storms, Floods, Earthquakes, Landslides, Avalanches, Heat waves, Cyclones, Volcanic Eruptions, Tsunamis, Cold waves, Droughts, Epidemics & pandemic, Hailstorms, Technological and biological hazards, (agents - micro-organisms, insect of infestation). Natural disasters cannot be prevented, but measures can be taken to eliminate or reduce the possibility of trouble. Regardless of the many forms a disaster may take, the actual damage to the collection is usually caused by fire or water. Even when they are not the initial factor, fires and floods almost invariably occur as secondary causes of library and archive disasters. Manmade Disaster Acts of War and Terrorism – Destruction, looting, or displacement of collections during conflict or violent unrest. Water Damage – Caused by broken pipes, leaking roofs, blocked drains, or water used in fire extinguishing. Fire Hazards – Including accidental or deliberate fires, explosions, and power failures that may trigger electrical sparks. Chemical Hazards – Spillage of liquid chemicals or exposure to harmful cleaning/preservation agents. Structural Deficiencies – Risks arising from poor design, weak construction, inadequate environmental controls, or lack of regular maintenance in buildings housing manuscripts. Intentional Disasters These refer to deliberate human actions that pose a direct threat to the safety and preservation of manuscripts: Vandalism – The purposeful defacement, mutilation, or destruction of manuscripts or archival infrastructure. Arson – The intentional setting of fire to cause extensive damage to collections and storage facilities. Bomb Attacks – Explosive assaults aimed at cultural institutions, leading to irreversible loss of manuscripts and heritage. Disaster Plan Libraries and archives are vulnerable to a wide range of risks, including natural disasters, accidents, technical failures, and human negligence. A well-structured disaster plan ensures not only the protection of collections but also the continuity of operations during unforeseen events. Such a plan provides a systematic framework for preventing damage, preparing for emergencies, responding effectively, and recovering from losses in a timely and efficient manner. The disaster management cycle generally encompasses four phases: 1 Prevention 2 Preparedness 3 Response 4 Recovery Prevention The prevention phase focuses on identifying risks, minimizing hazards, and safeguarding collections through proactive measures. Key Actions: Conduct building inspections to identify and mitigate potential hazards from structures, equipment, and fittings. Establish routine housekeeping and maintenance practices to ensure resilience of buildings and surroundings. Install automatic fire detection and extinguishing systems, water-sensing alarms, smoke detectors, dust detectors, and air curtains. Use modular compact storage systems for manuscript preservation. Prepare acid-free, age-resistant archival storage boxes for rare manuscripts, protecting them from light, dust, and atmospheric impact. Install automatic fire detection and extinguishing systems, and water-sensing alarms, smoke detectors, and dust detectors of an air curtain. Employ U.V.-protected and anti-glare glass for display showcases. Take special precautions during high-risk periods, such as renovations. Provide additional protection for manuscripts on exhibition. Install environmental control and monitoring systems (for temperature, humidity, dust, and pollutants). Create security copies of vital records (e.g., collection inventories) and store them off-site. Maintain comprehensive insurance coverage for the building, collections, salvage operations, and restoration efforts. Provide security copies of vital records, such as collection inventories, and store these off-site. Do’s & Don’ts Check weather warnings regularly and identify safe zones inside and outside the building. Practice earthquake drills at the workplace, laboratories, repositories, home, and schools. Secure loose items that may fall, slide, or collide. Observe warning signs in natural and built environments. Stay informed about the weather, especially in heavy rainfall. Monitor regular weather conditions. Take precautions during heatwaves, marked by unusually high temperatures and humidity. pages.title: Faq
pages.text: Faq close close close close close close Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) What is Gyan Bharatam? Gyan Bharatam is a national mission led by the Ministry of Culture to digitally preserve, catalog, and share India’s rich manuscript heritage with the world. What types of manuscripts does Gyan Bharatam preserve? We preserve ancient manuscripts in various formats—palm-leaf, birch bark, paper—and in multiple languages, including Sanskrit, Persian, regional languages, and more. How can I search for manuscripts on the website? You can search by theme, script, region, or keyword using the homepage search bar to explore India's timeless knowledge. How does Gyan Bharatam ensure manuscript preservation? Manuscripts are conserved using scientific methods, digitized using sustainable technology, and protected by trained experts. Can I contribute or share an old manuscript I possess? Yes! If you hold a piece of India’s heritage, use our digitisation requests page. We’ll help you document, digitize, and preserve your manuscript. What events and workshops does Gyan Bharatam organize? We host workshops, conferences, webinars, and academic exchange programs to promote research and awareness about India’s manuscript culture. Can I access publications or research on Indian manuscripts? Yes—explore our Publications section for books, research papers, and digital editions. How can I contact Gyan Bharatam? Contact us via email (gyanbharatam2025@gmail.com), phone (+91-11-23315625), or social media (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, YouTube). Who can access the digitized manuscripts? The digital manuscripts are accessible to researchers, students, and the general public interested in India’s cultural heritage. Are there special collections or featured manuscripts on the website? Yes. We highlight unique manuscripts, including the Manuscript of the Week, notable works in science, literature, and more. What is the “Five Pillars” approach of Gyan Bharatam? Our work is based on five pillars: Survey & Cataloguing, Conservation & Capacity Building, Technology & Digitization, Linguistics & Translation, and Research, Outreach & Publication. How are manuscripts identified for preservation? Manuscripts are mapped and recorded across India through standardized national surveys and collaboration with local institutions. Do you offer educational resources for students or researchers? Yes. We provide study materials, curated book lists, and details about participating in manuscript studies and conferences. Can I request the digitisation of a manuscript in my possession? Absolutely! Use our “Get in Touch” or digitisation request links to submit details. Our team will guide you through the process. Are there resources available for various languages or scripts? Yes. Manuscripts are available in multiple languages and scripts, and our catalog supports theme/script/region-based searches. What partnerships does Gyan Bharatam have? We work with government agencies, academic institutions, heritage organizations, and UNESCO for projects, research, and recognition. How do you ensure the authenticity of cataloged manuscripts? Manuscripts are authenticated by expert teams using established scholarly, scientific, and archival standards. Is there a way to join or contribute to the Gyan Bharatam mission? Yes! Researchers, scholars, and heritage enthusiasts can participate via our workshops, volunteer opportunities, and collaborative projects. How can I stay updated on news or developments? Check our Press Releases section and follow us on social media for latest activities, events, and new publications. Are there privacy and accessibility options on the site? Our website offers accessibility features (e.g., dyslexia/ADHD modes, color options, text-to-speech) and follows strict privacy guidelines detailed in our Privacy Policy. analysis.overview: Gyan Bharatam is a national mission led by the Ministry of Culture to digitally preserve, catalog, and share India’s rich manuscript heritage with the world. It aims to safeguard and revitalize India’s Manuscript Heritage through systematic survey and documentation, scientific conservation, digitization, and publication, along with the creation of a national digital repository (NDR). The initiative was envisioned by the Honorable Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi, and the Hon’ble Finance Minister announced it in the Union Budget 2025–26. The Standing Finance Committee (SFC) has sanctioned Rs 491.66 crore for the period 2025–2031.
objectives: Safeguard and revitalize India’s Manuscript Heritage through systematic survey and documentation
Conserve manuscripts through scientific methods for preservation while training experts to care for them
Digitize and protect manuscripts with smart, sustainable technology for the future
Work with academicians & scholars to decode ancient manuscripts through translation and transliteration
Study and share knowledge of manuscripts through publication of critically edited manuscripts
Ensure accessibility of the National Digital Repository for research, education, and public engagement
analysis.eligibility: Open to individuals and institutions holding manuscripts, including citizen contributors, scholars, libraries, monasteries, temples, and academic institutions across India who wish to participate in the preservation, digitization, and sharing of manuscript heritage.
analysis.benefits: Access to digitized manuscripts for research, education, and public engagement; opportunity to register as a Gyan Bharatam Scholar; participation in workshops, conferences, and webinars; collaboration with researchers across India; access to manuscript repositories; contribution to national digital heritage infrastructure; recognition as part of a living network of seekers, scholars, teachers, and knowledge-keepers.
analysis.financial_support: The Standing Finance Committee (SFC) has sanctioned Rs 491.66 crore for the period 2025–2031 to support the operations of Gyan Bharatam, including survey, conservation, digitization, and creation of the National Digital Repository.
analysis.application_process: 1. Visit the Gyan Bharatam website at https://gyanbharatam.com/ 2. Navigate to the 'Register as Scholar' section via the homepage or community page 3. Fill out the scholar registration form with personal and institutional details 4. Submit the form to join the Gyan Bharatam Parivar network 5. Upon approval, gain access to manuscript repositories and collaborative opportunities.
analysis.deadlines: Rolling basis — no fixed deadline. Applications are accepted year-round.
analysis.implementing_agency: Ministry of Culture, Government of India
analysis.contact_details: Email: gyanbharatam2025@gmail.com | Phone: +91-11-23315625 | Shri Samar Nanda, Joint Secretary: +91 11 23386504 | Shri Inderjeet Singh, Director: +91 11 24014229 | Prof. Anirban Dash, Project Director: +91 11 23315625
caveats: Manuscript conservation and digitization efforts are subject to institutional partnerships and MoUs
Access to certain manuscript collections may be restricted based on custodian policies
Financial support is allocated for the period 2025–2031 and subject to government approvals

  "overview": "Gyan Bharatam is a national mission led by the Ministry of Culture to digitally preserve, catalog, and share India’s rich manuscript heritage with the world. It aims to safeguard and revitalize India’s Manuscript Heritage through systematic survey and documentation, scientific conservation, digitization, and publication, along with the creation of a national digital repository (NDR). The initiative was envisioned by the Honorable Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi, and the Hon’ble Finance Minister announced it in the Union Budget 2025–26.
